description: Pre-PRD backlog for the Ralph framework. Items here track learnings, unsolved problems, and future improvements that need attention but aren't ready to execute yet. Every item preserves WHY it was identified.

types.improvement: We know what to do, just haven't done it yet
types.unsolved_problem: We know this is an issue but don't have a solution design
types.working_knowledge: Lessons learned from real use — parked until we know where they belong in the framework

items.title: Context compaction recovery
items.description: Create a features/ directory for optional behaviors that can be turned on/off. First feature: context compaction recovery. Referenced from seed.md with a one-liner.
items.why: After context compaction, agents resumed wrong activities because compacted summaries lost nuance. This is not a philosophy — it's a concrete behavior to enable. Needs a home distinct from principles.
items.example_content: After context compaction, check the transcript JSONL. Compacted summaries can be misleading — read the last few messages to verify you're resuming the right activities. Cap reads to avoid flooding context.

items.title: Knowledge hygiene principles
items.description: Principles for writing durable knowledge files. Could live in seed.md or a new knowledge-guidelines.md.
items.why: Over ~20 PRD executions, agents wrote knowledge files with guesses, filler, and assumptions that misled future sessions.
items.example_content:
- No guesses in durable knowledge. Only confirmed facts. If uncertain, mark as unknown or put in task context.
- Concise and matter-of-fact. No fluff. Short bullets over paragraphs.
- Say "TBD" or "Unknown" explicitly. Don't fill gaps with assumptions.
- Unknowns go in tasks or TODOs, not knowledge files.

items.title: Don't duplicate mutable state in CLAUDE.md
items.description: Principle about what NOT to put in CLAUDE.md. Could live in seed.md or CLAUDE.md template guidance.
items.why: PRD status table was duplicated in both CLAUDE.md and plan.md. Agents updated one but not the other, causing repeated conflicts and stale information that misled subsequent sessions.
items.example_content: Never duplicate information in CLAUDE.md that is maintained by agents elsewhere (PRD status tables, task lists). It causes merge conflicts and staleness. Point to the source of truth instead.

items.title: Architect Test principle in seed.md
items.description: Add the PRD authoring principle ('you are NOT the architect — define the problem space richly') to seed.md as a working principle. Currently only in PRD_REFINE.md which is a review-time check, but the principle governs all task writing.
items.why: Agents writing PRD tasks without this principle consistently over-prescribed implementation details (specific classes, data structures, file layouts), leaving architects with nothing to decide. The principle needs to be internalized before writing, not just caught during review.
items.source: Orchestrator CLAUDE.md, PRD Authoring section; ralph-context/prds/PRD_REFINE.md
items.example_content:
When writing PRD tasks, you are NOT the architect. Your job is to define the problem space richly enough that the architect has real decisions to make. Never prescribe patterns, classes, data structures, file layouts, or communication protocols in task descriptions.

Test every task you write: Would an architect reading this have genuine architectural decisions to make? If not, you've either under-visioned (too thin) or over-prescribed (did their job).

items.title: Invariants section in CLAUDE.md template
items.description: Add an ## Invariants section placeholder to the CLAUDE.md template. Subsumes CLAUDE.md maintenance guidance ('only add insights that would be lost'). The template should prime projects to separate non-negotiable constraints from general guidelines.
items.why: The POC's pattern of separating non-negotiable constraints from guidelines changed agent behavior — invariants were treated with more weight than rules mixed into prose. Without this separation, agents occasionally violated critical constraints that were buried in general guidance.
items.example_content:
## Invariants

These are non-negotiable. Every change must respect these.

[Project-specific invariants go here. Each should state the rule AND why it exists.]

items.title: Preserving 'why' behind agent instructions
items.description: When agent instructions change (CLAUDE.md, seed.md, perspectives), the reasoning behind rules gets lost. Need a pattern where non-negotiables carry both the rule AND the incident/learning that produced it. Without the 'why', future editors can't judge whether a rule still applies or is safe to remove.
items.why: Observed during POC — invariants without rationale were sometimes weakened by agents who didn't understand their origin. This is also a meta-principle: the framework itself needs to practice what it preaches about preserving context.

items.title: Setup & Running section in CLAUDE.md template
items.description: Add ## Setup & Running section to CLAUDE.md template with placeholder for exact commands. /discover already looks for this but the template should prime it.
items.why: Without explicit run commands, agents kept re-discovering the same failures (wrong port, missing env var, wrong test command). Each session burned 5-10 minutes on setup archaeology that could have been avoided with a few lines in CLAUDE.md.
items.example_content:
## Setup & Running

```bash
# First-time setup
[commands here]

# Running
[commands here]

# Testing
[commands here]
```

items.title: Branch strategy configuration
items.description: Ralph defaults to one-branch-per-PRD. Some projects need a single long-lived branch. Currently handled via ad-hoc CLAUDE.md overrides. Needs a principled configuration mechanism.
items.why: The Ask Anything POC couldn't use per-PRD branches because the working branch was a namespace within a repo we don't own. The override worked but was fragile — agents sometimes created branches anyway despite the CLAUDE.md instruction.
items.example_content: Branch override: The Ralph process says 'one branch per PRD' but this project uses a single long-lived branch per repo. All PRD work lands on that branch. Do not create new branches per PRD.
